title: C# SOLID — Paper 3
description: Covers OCP concepts, OCP refactoring, LSP concepts, LSP refactoring, ISP concepts, Factory & Abstract Factory, Decorator Pattern, Observer Pattern, Repository Pattern, and Code Smells & SOLID Violations.

questions.text: ST07-1: The Interface Segregation Principle states that:

options.text: Clients should implement all methods of a large, general-purpose interface.
Clients should not be forced to depend on interfaces they do not use.
Interfaces should always be implemented by at least three classes.
All interface methods must have default implementations.

questions.text: ST16-4: The Repository pattern improves testability primarily because:

options.text: It forces all queries to be written in raw SQL.
It hides the ORM, preventing tests from running.
The IRepository interface can be mocked or faked, eliminating the need for a real database in unit tests.
It automatically generates test data.

questions.text: ST03-7: Which of the following is NOT a valid way to achieve OCP in C#?

options.text: Using interfaces with multiple implementations.
Using abstract classes with virtual methods.
Directly editing a stable, published class to add a new feature.
Using the Strategy pattern to inject new behaviour.

questions.text: ST13-7: When should you prefer a simple Factory Method over an Abstract Factory?

options.text: When you need to create several families of related products.
When you need to create only one type of product and the creation logic needs to be subclassed.
When you need dependency injection built into the factory.
When you want to prevent any subclassing of the product.

questions.text: ST17-1: Which code smell describes a class that has grown to handle nearly all responsibilities in a system?

options.text: Feature Envy
Shotgun Surgery
God Class (Large Class)
Divergent Change

questions.text: ST05-7: Which design guideline follows directly from LSP: 'favour composition over inheritance'?

options.text: It makes classes easier to serialize.
Composition avoids the tight subtype contract that inheritance imposes, reducing the risk of LSP violations.
Composition always runs faster than inheritance.
It removes the need for interfaces entirely.

questions.text: ST14-6: Which of the following correctly describes how Decorator differs from Inheritance for adding behaviour?

options.text: Inheritance adds behaviour at runtime; Decorator adds it at compile time.
Decorator adds behaviour at runtime by wrapping; Inheritance adds it at compile time through subclassing.
Both Decorator and Inheritance add behaviour at the same time.
Decorator uses reflection, while Inheritance uses code generation.

questions.text: ST06-5: A `ReadOnlyCollection<T>` derived from `Collection<T>` throws `NotSupportedException` on calls to `Add()`. Regarding LSP, this is:

options.text: Acceptable because it documents the exception in XML comments.
A violation because code expecting a writable Collection<T> cannot safely substitute ReadOnlyCollection<T>.
Compliant because throwing exceptions is always allowed in overrides.
Compliant because .NET itself does this in the BCL.

questions.text: ST04-7: Template Method is an OCP-supporting pattern. What is its structure?

options.text: A base class defines the overall algorithm as a series of steps; subclasses override specific steps without changing the algorithm skeleton.
A context class delegates all algorithm selection to a strategy object.
A factory creates algorithm objects based on runtime configuration.
An interface declares all algorithm steps, and clients choose which to call.

questions.text: ST15-7: Which of the following is an advantage of using C# events over exposing a public list of delegates for Observer?

options.text: Events are faster than lists of delegates.
Events enforce encapsulation: only the publisher can raise the event; subscribers can only add/remove handlers.
Events automatically remove dead subscribers.
Events support generic type parameters, lists do not.

questions.text: ST03-8: An IAnimal interface has Eat(), Sleep(), Fly(), and Swim(). A Dog class must implement Fly() by throwing NotSupportedException. Which principle does this violate?

options.text: OCP — because IAnimal is not open for extension.
ISP — because Dog is forced to implement a method it does not use.
SRP — because IAnimal has too many responsibilities.
DIP — because IAnimal is a concrete type.

questions.text: ST07-2: A 'fat' interface is one that:

options.text: Has a very large number of implementing classes.
Contains more methods than any single client needs to use.
Uses generic type parameters extensively.
Inherits from more than one base interface.

questions.text: ST13-8: Factory patterns support OCP by:

options.text: Preventing any new product types from being added.
Centralising object creation so new product types can be added by extending the factory, not modifying client code.
Making all product constructors private.
Requiring all products to implement the same base class.

questions.text: ST06-6: Which refactoring resolves an LSP violation caused by a subclass that strengthens a precondition (e.g., rejects null while the base accepts it)?

options.text: Remove the override and call the base method directly.
Redesign: either relax the subclass precondition, or extract the subclass into an unrelated class that does not inherit from the base.
Add a null-check to the base class method.
Seal the base class so it cannot be subclassed.

questions.text: ST17-2: 'Feature Envy' is a code smell where:

options.text: A class has too many fields.
A method is more interested in the data of another class than its own.
Two classes share the same interface.
A method is duplicated in multiple classes.

questions.text: ST05-8: Barbara Liskov's original formulation of LSP is stated in terms of:

options.text: Design by Contract — preconditions, postconditions, and invariants.
GRASP — General Responsibility Assignment Software Patterns.
UML class diagrams showing inheritance hierarchies.
Test-Driven Development principles.

questions.text: ST16-5: A Generic Repository pattern in C# typically looks like:

options.text: IRepository with methods specific to each entity type in one large interface.
IRepository<T> with generic methods such as GetById, GetAll, Add, Update, Delete.
A static class with entity-specific static methods.
DbContext subclass with entity-specific DbSet properties exposed directly.

questions.text: ST04-8: Which of the following correctly describes the role of an abstract class in supporting OCP?

options.text: It is closed for extension because it cannot be instantiated.
It provides a stable base (closed for modification) while allowing concrete subclasses to extend behaviour (open for extension).
Abstract classes violate OCP because they expose implementation details.
Abstract classes and OCP are unrelated concepts.

questions.text: ST07-3: 'Role interfaces' in ISP refer to:

options.text: Interfaces that are named after their implementing classes.
Small, focused interfaces that each represent a specific role or capability a client requires.
Interfaces used only in security and authorization contexts.
Interfaces that contain only one abstract method.

questions.text: ST14-7: In a C# coffee shop application, a plain Coffee class implements ICoffee. A MilkDecorator and a SugarDecorator each also implement ICoffee and wrap an ICoffee instance. A client orders a coffee with milk and sugar. How are the decorators applied?

options.text: new Coffee() is subclassed twice to produce MilkSugarCoffee.
new SugarDecorator(new MilkDecorator(new Coffee())) — decorators are stacked.
Coffee.AddMilk() and Coffee.AddSugar() are called directly.
A CoffeeBuilder constructs the decorated coffee in a single step.

questions.text: ST03-9: Which statement about the OCP and unit tests is most accurate?

options.text: OCP-compliant code is harder to unit test because of abstraction layers.
When code respects OCP, new behaviour can be added (and tested) in new classes without risking regression in existing tested code.
OCP has no impact on testing strategies.
OCP requires modifying tests every time new behaviour is added.

questions.text: ST17-3: 'Shotgun Surgery' is a code smell where:

options.text: A single class changes for many different reasons.
A single change requires editing many different classes.
Many classes share the same method signature.
A method contains too many parameters.

questions.text: ST06-7: Which of the following overriding behaviors in a subclass is fully LSP compliant?

options.text: Logging the call before delegating to the base implementation.
Returning null where the base contract guarantees a non-null return.
Throwing an ArgumentException for inputs the base class accepts.
Silently ignoring all work that the base method performs.

questions.text: ST05-9: A subtype S is a subtype of T according to LSP if:

options.text: S has more methods than T.
For all objects x of type S, every property provable of x by T also holds for objects of type S.
S inherits from T and seals all inherited methods.
T is an abstract class and S is a concrete implementation.

questions.text: ST15-8: In C#, `EventHandler<TEventArgs>` is a built-in delegate type. What must a subscriber method's signature look like to be compatible?

options.text: void Handler(TEventArgs e)
void Handler(object sender, TEventArgs e)
bool Handler(object sender, TEventArgs e)
Task Handler(object sender, TEventArgs e)

questions.text: ST13-9: Which problem does the Abstract Factory pattern most directly solve?

options.text: Ensuring a class has only one instance.
Ensuring that a set of related objects is created together consistently, without coupling code to specific concrete classes.
Converting the interface of a class into another interface clients expect.
Defining the steps of an algorithm in a base class.

questions.text: ST07-4: Which of the following is a sign that ISP is being violated?

options.text: An interface has three methods and three implementing classes.
Implementing classes routinely throw NotImplementedException for several interface methods.
An interface is used by only one class.
An interface is defined in a separate assembly.

questions.text: ST04-9: In C#, which language feature was introduced to support OCP at the interface level by providing a fallback implementation?

options.text: Abstract methods
Default interface methods (C# 8.0+)
Static constructors
Partial classes

questions.text: ST16-6: A Unit of Work pattern is often paired with the Repository pattern. Its primary role is to:

options.text: Replace the repository interface entirely.
Track all changes made via repositories during a business transaction and commit them as a single atomic operation.
Cache repository results in a distributed cache.
Generate SQL queries automatically from LINQ expressions.

questions.text: ST17-4: 'Divergent Change' is a code smell that indicates a violation of:

options.text: The Open/Closed Principle, because the class must change for every new requirement.
The Single Responsibility Principle, because the class changes for multiple different reasons.
The Interface Segregation Principle, because the class implements too many interfaces.
The Liskov Substitution Principle, because the class overrides base methods incorrectly.
